nativeRange.habitat: Sandy and sandy-muddy substrates in open water and along the margins of Lake Malawi; found over sand flats at intermediate depths, often following sand-sifting cichlids and eartheaters to exploit disturbed substrates

decorPreferences: deep open sand substrate (fine sand, minimum 7–10 cm depth for sand-sifting)
minimal rockwork — open swimming lanes essential
smooth rocks or driftwood along tank margins for territory definition
avoid sharp-edged decor that may injure the nuchal hump
strong filtration to handle heavy bioload

diet.notes: In the wild, the Blue Dolphin Cichlid feeds primarily on invertebrates, small crustaceans, and organic matter sifted from sandy substrates, closely following other sand-disturbing species such as Taeniolethrinops and Aulonocara to pick up what they expose. In the aquarium it readily accepts high-quality cichlid pellets, frozen bloodworms, brine shrimp, mysis shrimp, and krill, supplemented occasionally with chopped earthworms or silversides. A quality pellet with a spirulina component should form the dietary foundation rather than a heavy protein-only regime. Beef heart and other mammalian meats should be avoided, as these have been strongly associated with Malawi Bloat, a serious and often fatal digestive condition seen in African cichlids.

goodWith: other large Lake Malawi Haplochromines of similar size
large Synodontis catfish (S. multipunctatus, S. petricola)
non-aggressive mbuna such as Labidochromis caeruleus in large tanks
avoidWith: aggressive mbuna (Melanochromis, Pseudotropheus crabro — excessive harassment)
very small fish and nano species (may be swallowed or harassed)
other hump-headed haps such as Placidochromis milomo (territory conflict)
South American or Central American cichlids (incompatible water chemistry and aggression mismatch)
invertebrates (shrimp and snails will be consumed)
conspecific males without sufficient tank length — male-male aggression intensifies at breeding

imageCandidates.notes: Adult male showing full nuchal hump and brilliant cobalt-blue coloration. FishBase species photo.
Adult male; good lateral profile showing hump. Confirm license on page before use.

summary: The Blue Dolphin Cichlid (Cyrtocara moorii) is a large Lake Malawi cichlid best known for the pronounced forehead hump that develops on adult males. Juveniles are drab grey, but males mature into a brilliant cobalt blue. Keeping this species well requires 400 litres or more of hard, alkaline water and a deep sandy substrate.
careNotes:
The Blue Dolphin Cichlid (Cyrtocara moorii) is endemic to Lake Malawi and belongs to the Haplochromine group — commonly called ‘Haps’ in the hobby — which includes the peacock cichlids (Aulonocara). Unlike the mbuna, the notoriously combative rock-dwelling cichlids of the same lake, C. moorii is comparatively placid for its size. That said, males tend to become territorial toward one another and toward similarly shaped haps when breeding condition sets in, so tankmates and layout need to account for this.

The minimum recommended tank is 400 litres with a footprint of approximately 150 cm, providing enough territory and open sand for natural foraging. Many experienced keepers recommend 500 litres or more for a colony or mixed Hap-Peacock display. Water chemistry should mirror Lake Malawi conditions: hard (gH 10–25), alkaline (pH 7.5–8.5), and well-buffered (kH 10–20), at a temperature of 24–28°C. Frequent partial water changes of 25–30% per week are important given the high waste output of these large cichlids.

A deep fine-sand substrate of at least 7 cm is essential. In the wild, C. moorii scoops up mouthfuls of sand and strains invertebrates through its gill rakers (comb-like structures in the throat that act as a filter), and it will perform this same behaviour in the aquarium. The sand also cushions the fish’s forehead during feeding and resting contact with the bottom.

The most distinctive feature of adult males is the pronounced nuchal hump — the bulge on the forehead that gives the species its common names. This hump develops gradually from around 12–15 cm in length and is more prominent in dominant, well-conditioned males. Juveniles and females lack the hump entirely and are a drab grey; young fish are sometimes sold in shops without a clear identification, and the full transformation to brilliant cobalt blue typically takes 18–24 months. Only one dominant male should be kept unless the aquarium is very large (600 litres or more).

Malawi Bloat is the primary health risk for this species. It is strongly associated with diets high in mammalian protein (such as beef heart) and with deteriorating water quality. Feeding a quality cichlid pellet as the dietary staple and maintaining a consistent water change schedule are the most effective preventive measures.
breedingNotes:
The Blue Dolphin Cichlid is a maternal mouthbrooder, meaning the female incubates the eggs inside her mouth rather than in a nest. After spawning on a flat sand patch or smooth rock surface, she collects the eggs and holds them in her mouth for approximately 21–28 days. During this period she refuses food and her jaw appears visibly distended. Spawning in a well-conditioned aquarium is not uncommon, but successfully rearing fry requires either a dedicated species tank or removal of the holding female to a separate brooding vessel, since fry are at significant risk of predation from tankmates.

The recommended colony ratio is one male to two or three females; a 1:3 ratio or wider reduces the male’s tendency to harass females after spawning. Males may display aggressively toward other males and toward similarly shaped haps during courtship. Fry are released at approximately 10–12 mm and will accept micro-pellets, baby brine shrimp, and finely crushed flake from their first feeding. A brood of 20–50 fry is typical. Breeding is considered moderately difficult, primarily because of the tank-size requirement and the need to protect holding females from disturbance.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Cyrtocara moorii
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size, temperature range (24–28°C), pH (7.5–8.5), hardness, native range (Lake Malawi), biotope, diet (invertivore/sand-sifter), compatibility notes, and breeding (maternal mouthbrooder, 21–28 day incubation). Tank size minimum 350–400 L with 150 cm footprint confirmed.

additional.name: FishBase — Cyrtocara moorii
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (to 25 cm TL males), temperature range, taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes), native range (Lake Malawi — Malawi, Mozambique, Tanzania), depth range (3–15 m), and diet classification (invertivore). Confirmed monotypic genus.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Haplochromine Cichlid Care
additional.notes: General Hap care guidance used for tank size, water change regimen, and diet recommendations (cichlid pellet-based diet, avoidance of mammalian protein/Malawi Bloat risk). Species not individually listed but Hap-Peacock compatibility matrix applies directly.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Cyrtocara moorii profile
additional.notes: Supplementary source. Confirmed nuchal hump development timeline, juvenile coloration (drab grey), adult male brilliant blue, recommended group ratio (1 male : 2–3 females), and minimum tank size guidance. Also confirmed sand-follower foraging ecology.

additional.name: Cichlid-Forum — Cyrtocara moorii species profile
additional.notes: Community expert profile corroborating mouthbrooder incubation period (21–28 days), fry size at release (~10–12 mm), brood size (20–50 fry), and sand-sifting follower behaviour with Aulonocara and Taeniolethrinops in the wild. Also noted conspecific male aggression and hump prominence as dominance indicator.

fish.breedingNotes: The female incubates eggs inside her mouth for approximately 21–28 days, releasing free-swimming fry at around 10–12 mm. Spawning is achievable in home aquaria with a 1:2–3 male-to-female ratio. Fry are vulnerable to predation, so the holding female is ideally moved to a separate brooding tank. Brood size is typically 20–50.
